Holdings and characteristics are subject to change. Percentages may be rounded.

Market Overview

US fixed-income markets generated positive total returns during the first quarter, as measured by the Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index. Trade tensions and monetary-policy divergence drove market volatility and an increasingly clouded economic outlook. Most fixed-income sectors underperformed US Treasuries as spreads widened amid an expected deteriorating growth/inflation tradeoff.

The Bloomberg Municipal Bond 1-15 Year Blend (1-17) Index returned 0.39% during the quarter. The ratio of 10-year AAA general obligations (GOs) to 10-year Treasuries increased from 68.4% to 77.4%, remaining below the long-term historical average of 88% over the last 20 years.

Performance Summary

The Hartford Sustainable Municipal Bond Fund (I Share) outperformed the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index over the period.

The primary contributor to relative returns was security selection in investment-grade (IG) and high-yield revenue bonds, particularly in the healthcare, education, and utilities sectors.

The Fund’s security selection in GO bonds, specifically in local GOs, positively impacted performance over the period.

Duration* and yield-curve positioning contributed to performance, as we maintained an overweight position in the 10-year segment of the curve, where yields rose in the AAA Municipal GO curve.

Our underweight allocation in IG revenue bonds, specifically in port, airport, and marina modestly detracted from benchmark-relative returns.

Positioning & Outlook

Fundamentals are broadly positive after generous direct and indirect Federal support and coming out of the pandemic.

Many municipal sectors have natural outlets for inflationary pressures, but the more labor- constrained sectors will be more challenged.

Expect municipals to behave defensively in a downturn given their high quality and strong balance sheets.

Tax-exempt municipal valuations as proxied by the Muni/TSY ratio are unattractive relative to history. However, municipal bond all-in yields look attractive relative to comparable quality corporates at intermediate and long maturities.

Despite higher issuance this year, we expect demand to remain robust, particularly if rates begin to trend lower.

*Duration is a measure of the sensitivity of an investment’s price to nominal interest-rate movement.

data.title: Zoetis: Solid Q1 Results And A Weaker Dollar Provide Support (Upgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Zoetis (NYSE:ZTS) have been a disappointing performer over the past year, losing about 8% of their value. Since peaking in late 2024, shares have dropped about 20%. This adds to a period of long-term underperformance. I last covered shares back in 2022, rating the stock a “sell” on valuation concerns. Since then, ZTS has returned just 8% while the market has gained over 50%. However, periods of underperformance can come to an end. This week, Zoetis delivered solid earnings results and guidance. With updated financials, now is a good time to see if there is value opportunity in Zoetis. I am no longer a seller.

In the company’s first quarter, Zoetis earned $1.48, beating estimates by $0.08 on revenue of $2.2 billion, flat from last year. EPS was up 8% from last year, aided by a ~2% lower share count due to stock repurchases. Organic growth was up 9% given 5% volume growth and a 4% increase in prices. However, the stronger dollar was a significant headwind given the company gets over 40% of its revenue from overseas. That pressure will reverse substantially as the US dollar has weakened significantly since President Trump announced widespread tariffs. The dollar is down over 7% from its highs, which will mechanistically boost results of multinational companies like Zoetis.

Foreign exchange and tariffs create a lot of movement in Zoetis’s financials that are essentially out of its control. However, the company is managing its own operations fairly well. Even as revenue rose by 1%, the cost of sales fell by 3%, enabling 140bps of gross margin expansion. SG&A was up 2% to $554 million while it trimmed R&D spending by 2% to $157 million. I would not want to see R&D fall much further to ensure it continues investing sufficiently in its pipeline while SG&A cost control remains positive.

Looking at geographic results, its US business is about 55% of the company and saw 6% organic growth. Its pet business saw 8% growth to $965 million while livestock fell by 2% to $210 million. US gross margins expanded 190bs to 83.2%. Livestock was down an unadjusted 19% due to divestitures. A relatively weak pricing environment for livestock manufacturing is likely to keep this part of the business under pressure. Zoetis did receive a conditional license for its Avian flu vaccine in the US and Canada, which could provide some tailwind.

Its international segment saw 11% organic growth as its livestock unit performed much better than in the US, gaining 12% to $400 million. Foreign exchange was a 7% headwind to international results, which is why reported revenues were flat. Given the swing in the US dollar, this headwind should turn into a tailwind during the second quarter. International margins expanded 110bps to 70% and run persistently lower than the US given the higher of livestock business.

In terms of international exposure, I would note that China is just 6% of sales at $60 million. This was a 20% decline from last year. Conversely, Canada was the standout, growing by 17% to $67 million. I view the China tariffs and trade relationship as likely to be the most problematic, so this market will likely continue to be weak. I am also cautious that deeply damaged US-Canadian relations could cause growth to decelerate here.

Looking at its key franchises, its Simparica business continues to benefit from increased adoption of its “trio” product, benefitting from being the only dog parasiticide that can prevent flea tapeworms. It also gained approval in South Korea. This product group now accounts for about 18% of the company, growing 19%. Its Dermatology business was also up a solid 10%

Its osteoarthritis business is fairly small but is also growing very quickly. As the dog population ages, the potential cohort increases, and just 20% of dogs receive treatment. This is an area I am cautious on. As we have seen over the years, consumers, especially in the United States, love to spend money on their pets. However, in an environment where consumer sentiment is weakening and discretionary income may face pressure, we could see some consumers pull back. Products like anti-flea medication are likely to be resilient, but more discretionary “quality of life” treatments, like for OA, could see slower take-up in a weaker economy.

Because so much of its business is overseas, the weakness in the US dollar provides a significant opportunity, driving the $200 million increase in revenue guidance. I would note that it sees a 1% headwind from enacted tariffs to net income, suggesting it does not expect to fully pass through costs. It is important to note that this includes just enacted tariffs with most reciprocal tariffs under a 90 day pause. While there are likely to be some trade deals, tariffs will likely rise on at least some companies, meaning we may see future margin pressure. Even with slower adjusted net income growth, I believe EPS will be $0.20 higher, thanks to the FX tailwind.

In terms of Zoetis’s exposure to tariffs, it sees $20 million of tariff headwinds as explained on the earnings call. Its primary tariff exposure is livestock products from China. It sees greater risk from retaliatory tariffs. That is because 60% of its manufacturing is inside the US, and it is a net exporter. Most imports and exports are to Europe, where tariff rates are lower for now and never likely to reach the 145% China level. Separately, the Administration is considering sectoral tariffs on pharmaceuticals. It is not clear whether pharmaceutical tariffs will include pet products, but is hopeful they can be avoided. The motivation here does not seem as focused on pet medications, in my view.

I understand why the company is not issuing guidance beyond enacted tariffs as the future policy stance is unknowable. I expect the ultimate tariff impact to be 2-3x the current policy stance, creating another ~$40 million or $0.08 of risk to EPS. That actually still leaves the company a net beneficiary of the trade war, given the impact of the lower US dollar is likely to be over $0.20.

Zoetis also maintains an excellent balance sheet, ending Q1 with $1.7 billion of cash and just $6.6 billion of debt. That net debt position is just above 2x its $2.3 billion of 2024 free cash flow. As such, I expect all free cash flow to go to shareholders via its buyback 1.3% dividend yield. Indeed, there were over $400 million of Q1 buybacks.

Ultimately, the business continues to perform well, and Zoetis is a primary beneficiary of a weaker US dollar, more than offsetting any tariff costs. Even with incremental tariffs, I expect EPS to be at least $6.10 and for at least $2.4 billion of free cash flow. Shares are trading at about 25x earnings with a ~3.4% free cash flow yield ($2.4 billion of free cash flow over a ~$70 billion market cap). This is a high-margin business with excellent free cash flow that should benefit from increased spending on pets over time.

Still, this is not cheap, and ZTS shares have been a victim of excessive valuation. While shares are trading at a large discount to their own history, they remain very expensive relative to peers. I do believe this does limit ZTS’s upside potential, especially is discretionary consumer spending slows.

Given secular tailwinds, I do think ZTS does merit some premium valuation, especially as it has an under-levered balance sheet, which could allow it to increase buybacks further if shares continue to underperform. Over the medium term, ZTS should be able to grow by at least 5% per year in a constant currency environment, so a starting 3.4% free cash flow yield and 5% growth provide scope for an 8+% return, as ZTS can return all free cash flow to investors via dividends and buybacks given its strong balance sheet.

I struggle to get excited about this valuation, but I don’t think shares merit a sell anymore. I see ZTS as likely to be a market performer and hold up relatively well if tariffs escalate. If the dollar continues to weaken, that could open the door to more upside. Overall, ZTS is performing well, but valuation reflects this appropriately, leaving shares as a hold at current levels.

Market Overview

Global equities fell in the first quarter, as financial markets were rattled by seismic shifts in US foreign policy, which significantly altered the outlook for global trade, economic growth, and inflation. Consequently, capital rotated away from the US to international markets, particularly Europe, which outperformed amid signs of a more favorable economic backdrop. Among these signs were less restrictive monetary policy, improving leading indicators like manufacturing output, and greater fiscal stimulus. China’s economy expanded by 5% in 2024, meeting the government’s official target. Its State Council announced a special action plan to bolster consumption and the economy, including tax incentives and subsidies for key industries.

The MSCI ACWI ex US Growth index returned 1.96% for the quarter. Within the index, eight out of 11 sectors rose during the period. Communication services and materials were the top-performing sectors, while real estate and information technology were the bottom-performing sectors for the quarter.

Performance Summary

The Hartford International Growth Fund (I Share) underperformed the MSCI ACWI ex USA Growth Net Index during the quarter.

Weak selection in information technology, materials, and healthcare was partially offset by selection in industrials and financials.

Sector allocation, a result of our bottom-up stock selection process, detracted from relative results, driven by our overweight to information technology and underweight to materials. This was partially offset by our overweight to communication services.

Our top relative detractors were an out-of-benchmark position in Flutter Entertainment (consumer discretionary) and an overweight to Advantest (information technology).

Top two relative contributors were an overweight to Rheinmetall (industrials) and an out-of- benchmark position in Prudential (financials).

Positioning & Outlook

Despite potentially higher tariffs, European and Chinese equities outperformed. European stocks were driven by easing financial conditions, favorable policy changes, and low starting valuations while Chinese equities benefitted from increased corporate and consumer optimism and investor preference for domestic technology companies.

Despite a rotation away from companies levered to artificial-intelligence themes, we maintain exposure to names with competitive advantages in driving sustainable long-term growth trajectories. As the market rotates, we look for companies across sectors with strong fundamentals that leverage underappreciated market opportunities as tailwinds to inflecting growth.

At the end of the period, our largest overweights were to information technology and consumer discretionary. We were most underweight to consumer staples and materials.

data.title: U.S.-UK Is The First New Trade Deal, How Did We Do?
data.description:
Introduction

The morning of May 8th, a trade deal was signed between the United States and the United Kingdom. This comes as welcome news for the markets, which have been turbulent since "Liberation Day," or the unveiling of global proposed tariff rates.

There are too many moving parts to keep track of in trade policy, so I recommend taking a look at this tariff tracker from Bloomberg, as it does a great job of keeping everything organized and legible.

This deal is the first of many promises to come by the Trump administration, who famously (or infamously, depending on your political lean) said that they would do "90 deals in 90 days."

Now a little over 30 days in, and the results are beginning to flow, with the word that an agreement with India and the EU are coming soon as well.

The U.S.-UK Special Relationship

It's important to start any discussion of Anglo-American trade relations by noting out that the U.S. has historically had a very close relationship with the UK, which has historically been called the "special relationship." The phrase comes from a post-WW2 speech by Winston Churchill, then prime minister of the UK, but the partnership more recently blossomed in the 1980s, when then-Prime Minister Margaret Thatcher and President Ronald Reagan built out the neoliberal world order.

Flash forward to today, and the language being used by both sides still indicates that this relationship is important to both countries. In speaking about this deal, President Trump called Britain the U.S.' oldest ally, "just about." (Sorry, France.)

Because trade deals can't be enacted with EU countries without the approval of Brussels, and can only be done at the bloc level, the UK makes perfect sense to achieve the first deal, given this special relationship.

During the May 8th press conference in the UK, Prime Minister Kier Starmer evoked this phrase again:

As VE Day reminds us, the UK has no greater ally than the United States, so I am delighted that eight decades on, under President Trump the special relationship remains a force for economic and national security.

The Deal Has Been A Long Time Coming

There is an important timeline to discuss here that sets up some context. Prior to Brexit, the leaving of the European Union by the United Kingdom, the UK's trade relationship with the U.S. was governed by EU trade rules. Within the EU, member countries are forbidden from doing their own trade deals with external nations, and outsiders like the U.S. were made to do EU-wide deals. This falls in line with the EU "single market" doctrine, which aims to treat the entirety of the EU as one trade bloc externally, with free economic movement internally.

This is not dissimilar to how the U.S. operates, with the federal government oversees and enforcing intrastate commerce, and trade deals with foreign nations only able to take place at the national level. It would be very odd if California tried to make a trade deal with Mexico that excluded Arizona and Texas, for example.

While Brexit was a long-time coming from the referendum that triggered it in 2016, the country didn't leave the EU until 2020. Once it left in late January of that year, the UK attempted to negotiate new trade deals with its closest trading partners, like the U.S. However, no progress was made.

By the start of 2024, the UK still hadn't built out a U.S.-UK deal, and Kemi Badenoch, the leader of the Tories, the UK's center-right party, began to say that it was the U.S. holding up a deal, claiming the Biden administration had "no appetite for trade deals."

There's a Lot of Trade Already

Prior to this deal on May 8th, the U.S. was already the UK's largest trading partner, and accounted for nearly-18% of total UK trade, even without a Biden-era deal. On the subject, the British government writes:

Total trade in goods and services (exports plus imports) between the UK and United States was £314.6 billion in the four quarters to the end of Q4 2024, an increase of 3.9% or £11.9 billion in current prices from the four quarters to the end of Q4 2023. Of this £314.6 billion:

• Total UK exports to United States amounted to £196.3 billion in the four quarters to the end of Q4 2024 (an increase of 4.3% or £8.2 billion in current prices, compared to the four quarters to the end of Q4 2023);

• Total UK imports from United States amounted to £118.3 billion in the four quarters to the end of Q4 2024 (an increase of 3.2% or £3.7 billion in current prices, compared to the four quarters to the end of Q4 2023).

On the U.S. side, the UK is our 7th largest export destination, very close to the amount Germany and South Korea import from us. Interestingly enough, the UK is one of few countries that we have a trade surplus with, where they import more than they export. Trade has been growing YoY for every year over the last decade bar 2020, but that was a global issue.

Now that we have a new trade deal in hand, that begs the question: is it any good? If so, for whom?

U.S.-UK Trade Deal Details

Context: The full trade deal has not been inked yet, and so this announcement is more like a framework than a fully fledged trade deal. It will still be enforceable, but expect new details to emerge as the days and weeks go on.

As soon as the deal was announced, the British government posted a press release that provides more detail:

  • Car export tariffs will reduce from 27.5% to 10%.
    • This will apply to a quota of 100,000 cars, almost the total the UK exported last year.
  • Steel and aluminum tariffs from 25% to 0%.
    • This will apply to all steel and aluminum leaving the UK.
  • Aerospace and defense firms will see some exemptions to tariffs.
  • Reciprocal market access for beef and other agricultural products.
    • This will apply to a quote of 13,000 tons of beef p.a.
    • There will be no change in food standards for imports to the UK, and maintaining bans on hormone-fed beef and chlorinated chicken.
  • UK to get preferential treatment on all future tariffs related to specific goods or sectors, such as pharmaceuticals.
  • No change on the contentious "Digital Services Tax" for big tech firms in the UK.

For context, cars were the number one deal to iron out, as they are the largest export from the UK to the U.S.

Overall, this seems like a good deal for both parties. The British were primarily concerned with automakers and steel leading up to this meeting, and they got tariff-free access for steel. The partnership with U.S.-UK over materials makes a lot of sense geopolitically, as UK materials firms are not doing the same kind of "dumping" that has led the U.S. to seek out protectionist policies for its domestic steel industry.

Likewise with vehicles, British manufacturers, like Jaguar Land Rover and Aston Martin (OTCPK:ARGGY), are in high demand in the U.S. Getting those tariffs down to the base case for nations, the 10% global tariff rate, is very helpful for these firms as it will make them more competitive than EU-based firms that are still facing the higher rates.

A deal was also struck for the U.S. to import more Rolls-Royce (OTCPK:RYCEY) engines and airplane parts in exchange for the UK importing $10B worth of Boeing (BA) aircraft.

One losing point for the U.S. was the no change status for the Digital Services Tax, which is a charge of 2% of revenue on big tech firms operating in the UK. This is one of the larger sticking points for U.S. tech firms that operate overseas, and was expected to have some carve-outs made in this deal.

While that didn't happen, that was the only "sour" part of the deal for the U.S. side. This appears as a win-win currently, which is very positive for several firms directly involved in these deals.

Conclusion

Ultimately, this first deal appeared to be a win for the U.S. and the UK. While the U.S. didn't get everything it wanted, one almost never does in negotiations. Instead, what it got was a fair and honest deal that is working to deepen economic ties with the U.S.'s closest ally, which is a very welcome change of rhetoric from the hawkishness we've seen from the Trump Administration on Europeans.

If this deal is indicative of future deals made with allied nations like the UK, the U.S. should remain in a solid position to bend where it needs to (0% tariffs on UK steel) and be stiff where it can be (no firm deals on pharmaceuticals, only "preference").

The full details of the trade agreement will be telling, once those see the light of day, but it may be some time before everything is set in stone. For now, these rules are going to go in effect, and have already impressed the market.

Two caveats to that positive note are:

  • This likely only applies to our allies, whom we are far more likely to give preferential treatment. China will definitely not be afforded the same luxuries, given the U.S. classifies them as an "adversarial nation." This will make reaching a deal with them far tougher than the UK, potentially dragging out negotiations for some time.
  • An EU-wide deal will be harder to reach because it has a trade deficit with the U.S., unlike the UK which started from a better position on trade accounts, something President Trump has said informs his view of who is "winning" a given trade relationship.

However, I remain positive that follow-up deals with fall along these lines. The goal of much of these trade deals is to isolate adversarial nations, but also to re-align markets of allies. These objectives are not mutually exclusive, per se.

Thanks for reading.
